retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The phase composition and preparation method influence the chemical stability of hydroxyapatite. For instance, substituting apatite with magnesium, carbonate, or strontium increases solubility. [17] In contrast, substituting fluoride reduces solubility. Sintered hydroxyapatite has higher chemical stability than non-sintered forms, making it less soluble in vivo. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The dental role of hydroxyapatite is significant, as it constitutes 70% to 80% of the weight of dentin and enamel. Enamel, the hardest substance in the human body, is made up of relatively large hydroxyapatite crystals measuring 25 nm thick, 40 to 120 nm wide, and 160 to 1000 nm long. [6] Unlike bone, enamel lacks collagen; instead, amelogenins and enamelins provide a framework for mineralization. Hydroxyapatite is the primary component of enamel, helping to minimize diffuse reflectivity by filling surface pores, which contributes to the semitranslucent appearance of enamel. [4] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: 3. Setting reaction: The base paste containing hydrosilane-terminated molecules reacts with an accelerator paste containing siloxane oligomers with vinyl end groups and a platinum catalyst. Although no by-product is formed, a secondary reaction leads to hydrogen gas production. That is why it is recommended to wait at least 30 minutes rather than have a pitted cast. [19] 4. Advantages: Excellent dimensional stability - impressions can be stored or posted before casting. Elastic recovery Great accuracy Short setting time Good tear resistance Automix available Hydrophilized addition silicone has good compatibility with gypsum The impression can be cast multiple times without jeopardizing the details [20] 5. Disadvantages: Hydrophobic - the impression area must be dry to prevent inaccuracies in the impression [21] The sulfur present in the latex gloves and rubber dam may interfere with the polymerization of the base and catalyst. Hydrogen gas may lead to pitting in the cast.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Materials: Biodentine, a Calcium Silicate Bioactive -- Issues of Concern -- Microleakage
retrieved_docs.content: Biodentine has good marginal integrity because of its ability to form hydroxyapatite crystals, which improves sealing at the material-dentin interface. [4] Calcium silicate-based cement interacts with phosphate ions in saliva, forming apatite deposits, which can also increase sealing potential. [4] The nanostructure and slight material expansion contribute to better adaptation. [4] However, more research is needed to determine why leakage occurs with calcium silicate-based materials.

The base contains hydroxy-terminated polysiloxane polymer which undergoes crosslinking, and fillers to control viscosity. The accelerator contains divinylpolysiloxane prepolymers, which are crosslinking agents, chloroplatinic acid, which is a catalyst, palladium, which is a hydrogen absorber, and fillers

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: 4. Setting reaction: It sets in 2 steps: first, by the hydrolysis of ZnO to form zinc hydroxide (water is required to initiate the reaction). The second stage is a typical acid-base reaction in which zinc hydroxide reacts with eugenolic acid to form zinc eugenolate, forming a chelate and water as a byproduct. The final set structure consists of ZnO particles embedded in the matrix of zinc eugenolate. The impression paste's setting time can be altered in a clinical setup according to need, either decreased by adding a drop of water during mixing or increased by adding vaseline during mixing. The material has a good flow, which records fine details. ZOE's impression has excellent dimensional stability for 24 hrs; therefore, it can be poured within this time. However, the special impression tray underneath should not deform. [11] 5. Indications:

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Materials: Biodentine, a Calcium Silicate Bioactive -- Issues of Concern -- Setting Reaction and Interaction with Dentin
retrieved_docs.content: The setting reaction of Biodentine involves a hydration process, during which the powder and liquid components are mixed using an amalgamator. This trituration process results in a creamy paste. The reaction of the powder with the liquid forms a silicate hydrate gel and calcium hydroxide (CH). [1] Calcium hydroxide dissociates into hydroxyl (OH − ) and calcium ions (Ca 2+ ), increasing the pH and Ca 2+ concentrations. [1] This release of Ca 2+ contributes to the material's bioactivity and apatite-forming properties and stimulates dental pulp cell differentiation, ultimately promoting dentin bridge formation. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hydroxyapatite is an inorganic mineral with a typical apatite lattice structure represented as (A10(BO4)6C2), where A, B, and C correspond to Ca, PO 4 , and OH, respectively. Pure hydroxyapatite contains 39.68% calcium and 18% phosphorus by weight, resulting in a Ca/P molar ratio of 1.67. Commercial hydroxyapatite products have Ca/P ratios that can be higher or lower than 1.67. This variation in the Ca/P ratio indicates a phase shift between tricalcium phosphate (TCP) and calcium oxide (CaO). Hydroxyapatite with a Ca/P ratio greater than 1.67 contains more CaO than TCP, while those with a lower ratio contain more TCP. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Materials: Biodentine, a Calcium Silicate Bioactive -- Clinical Significance -- Clinical Applications of Biodentine: Primary Teeth Pulpotomy
retrieved_docs.content: The study results show high success rates for Biodentine and formocresol pulpotomy techniques over 12 months, which can be attributed to proper protocol, isolation, aseptic conditions, and material handling. [5] The success rates were consistent with earlier research, emphasizing that Biodentine can be a suitable alternative for formocresol in primary teeth pulpotomy. [5] Furthermore, the simultaneous use of Biodentine as a dressing and restorative material offers a clinical advantage over formocresol, which requires additional restorative material in the pulp chamber. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Materials: Biodentine, a Calcium Silicate Bioactive -- Issues of Concern -- Porosity and Material-Dentine Interface
retrieved_docs.content: Biodentine demonstrated lower porosity when used in a continuously moist environment, although it might present challenges in dry conditions. Research indicated that Biodentine adapts well to dentine due to micromechanical adhesion. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Another significant application of hydroxyapatite in dentistry dates back to 1979 when hydroxyapatite cylinders were used for tooth replacement. This application was followed in the early 1980s by the use of hydroxyapatite blocks and coatings to enhance bone fixation in restorative dental procedures. Hydroxyapatite is now used not only in dental cement and fillings but also in toothpaste, where it serves as a polisher to reduce the buildup of accretions on teeth. [14] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, the focus in hard tissue repair is on hydroxyapatite due to its significant chemical composition, which constitutes the majority of challenging tissue, and its mechanical properties that support tissue integrity. [8] Hydroxyapatite is widely utilized as an implant material because of its excellent osteoconductive properties that facilitate osseointegration and osteogenesis. The raw materials and synthesis processes of hydroxyapatite influence the biological response to its implants, resulting in variations in product properties.

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Synthetic and natural hydroxyapatite have long been preferred for hard tissue repair over autografts and allografts. This preference arises from issues commonly associated with grafts, such as graft shortages, donor site morbidity, disease transmission, and the risk of graft rejection.

Sodium monofluorophosphate is the active fluoride ingredient shared by the toothpastes sodium monofluorophosphate 0.0076 MG/MG Toothpaste, sodium monofluorophosphate 0.008 MG/MG Toothpaste, and sodium monofluorophosphate 0.0088 MG/MG Toothpaste, providing dental protection through caries prevention. In gastroenterology, diverticulosis and diverticulitis (رَتْجٌ مِعَوِيّ) are classified within the broader SMQ “الاختلال الوظيفي غير المحدد في الجهاز الهضمي” (unspecified functional gastrointestinal disorder). This SMQ also encompasses reflux esophagitis (الْتِهابُ المَريءِ الارتجاعي), diarrhea (جَيّار), dyspepsia (عُسْرُ الهَضْم), and gastroesophageal reflux disease (مرض الارتداد المَعِدِيّ المَريئِيّ). retrieved_docs.content: Monofluorophosphate 0.0076 MG/MG Toothpaste, 0.008 MG/MG Toothpaste, 0.0088 MG/MG Toothpaste) where it provides anticaries protection. In pharmacovigilance, the Standardized MedDRA Query titled “الاختلال الوظيفي غير المحدد في الجهاز الهضمي (SMQ)” groups a set of gastrointestinal disorders—reflux esophagitis, diarrhea, dyspepsia and gastro‑esophageal reflux disease—as manifestations of an unspecified functional gastrointestinal disturbance, facilitating systematic monitoring and analysis of related adverse events. Together, these points highlight the roles of magnesium chloride, calcium carbonate, and sodium monofluorophosphate in diverse therapeutic contexts, their mechanisms related to electrolyte activity or acid neutralization, and the organized categorization of associated gastrointestinal disorders for clinical safety assessment.

Sodium monofluorophosphate is distinct from calcium carbonate, which serves only as an inactive filler in those formulations. In the context of gastrointestinal disorders, the Standardized MedDRA Query “الاختلال الوظيفي غير المحدد في الجهاز الهضمي (SMQ)” groups related conditions such as الاضطراب المريئي الارتجاعي (reflux esophagitis), جَيّار (diarrhea), عُسْرُ الهَضْم (dyspepsia) and مرض الارتداد المَعِدِيّ المَريئِيّ (gastro‑esophageal reflux disease), illustrating how these diagnostic categories support precise reporting and therapeutic planning. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 175
retrieved_docs.content: with Calcium and Extra Iron. The mechanism of action attributed to calcium carbonate involves participation in electrolyte activity, modulation of calcium ion balance, and contribution to acid‑base homeostasis. Clinically it functions as an antacid for gastro‑esophageal reflux disease (GERD), reflux esophagitis, dyspepsia and diarrhea, providing symptomatic relief by neutralizing gastric acidity. Calcium carbonate is also noted as a downstream component of natural mineralized structures such as pearls. Sodium monofluorophosphate, the active fluoridated compound in the dental pastes, is present at concentrations from 0.22 µg/100 µg to 2.43 mg/g and is formulated together with calcium carbonate (0.0076–0.0088 mg/mg) to prevent dental caries. From a gastrointestinal classification perspective, diverticulosis and diverticulitis are grouped within the “unspecified functional gastrointestinal disorder” SMQ (الاختلال الوظيفي غير المحدد في الجهاز الهضمي SMQ), a category that also includes

Overall, the knowledge network connects caries‑preventive agents, fluoride‑based supplements, and various oral care preparations through shared classifications (stomatological preparations), chemical constituents (tin fluoride, sodium fluoride, sodium monofluorophosphate), and product‑level active‑ingredient relationships, underscoring the central role of fluoride compounds in dental prophylaxis and therapeutic oral formulations.

Recognizing sodium monofluorophosphate as a fluoride source guides oral‑health preventive strategies, while awareness of the SMQ classification aids clinicians in systematic identification and treatment of functional GI disturbances, underscoring the importance of these entities for patient care and product design.

The classification hierarchy links perleť (a pearl‑like calcium carbonate derivative) as a child of calcium carbonate. Sodium monofluorophosphate is the active fluoride compound in the dental pastes listed above and in specific toothpastes (0.0076 mg/mg, 0.008 mg/mg, 0.0088 mg/mg). Its preventive action against dental caries complements the excipient role of calcium carbonate in the same formulations. Beyond dental applications, calcium carbonate is recognized as a therapeutic antacid for functional gastrointestinal disorders classified within the unspecified functional GI disorder SMQ, including reflux esophagitis, diarrhea, dyspepsia and gastro‑esophageal reflux disease. Collectively, magnesium chloride, calcium carbonate and sodium monofluorophosphate illustrate the interconnected use of electrolytes and mineral compounds as active agents, stabilizing excipients, and therapeutic supplements across

It is present in specific toothpaste formulations (sodium monofluorophosphate 0.0076 mg/mg, 0.008 mg/mg, and 0.0088 mg/mg Toothpaste) and contributes to preventive oral health. In pharmacovigilance, the Standardized MedDRA Query “الاختلال الوظيفي غير المحدد في الجهاز الهضمي (SMQ)” aggregates four Arabic‑named gastrointestinal disorders—الْتِهابُ المَريءِ الارتجاعي (reflux esophagitis), جَيّار (diarrhea), عُسْرُ الهَضْم (dyspepsia), and مرض الارتداد المَعِدِيّ المَريئِيّ (GERD). Therapeutically, calcium carbonate is employed as an alkalinizing antacid for gastrointestinal disorders such as reflux esophagitis, diarrhea, dyspepsia and gastro‑esophageal reflux disease. Sodium monofluorophosphate is the active fluoride ingredient in the same dental pastes and toothpastes listed for calcium carbonate, providing caries‑preventive activity at the specified concentrations. The Standardized MedDRA Query for “unspecified functional gastrointestinal disorder” encompasses gastroesophageal reflux disease, diarrhea, dyspepsia and intestinal‑esophageal reflux disease, linking these functional GI disorders to the therapeutic use of calcium carbonate and emphasizing the importance of recognizing magnesium‑based agent

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 144

Recognizing sodium monofluorophosphate across multiple toothpaste products supports dental caries prevention, while grouping these GI entities under a single SMQ facilitates comprehensive coding, monitoring and clinical management of functional digestive disturbances.
